Price to Book of 10.8x: Is it justified?

Cogent Biosciences is trading on a P/B of 10.8x, which prices the stock very differently depending on whether you compare it with broad biotech peers or a narrower peer set.

The P/B ratio compares a company’s market value with its net assets on the balance sheet. For a clinical stage biotech with no meaningful revenue and a net loss of $328.9m, the market is often paying for the pipeline, management track record and future expectations rather than current earnings power.

data.title: Theon International (AMS:THEON) Strong Profits May Be Masking Some Underlying Issues
data.description:
Theon International Plc's (AMS:THEON) healthy profit numbers didn't contain any surprises for investors. We think this is due to investors looking beyond the statutory profits and being concerned with what they see.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

A Closer Look At Theon International's Earnings

Many investors haven't heard of the accrual ratio from cashflow, but it is actually a useful measure of how well a company's profit is backed up by free cash flow (FCF) during a given period. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. The ratio shows us how much a company's profit exceeds its FCF.

As a result, a negative accrual ratio is a positive for the company, and a positive accrual ratio is a negative. While having an accrual ratio above zero is of little concern, we do think it's worth noting when a company has a relatively high accrual ratio. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

Theon International has an accrual ratio of 0.23 for the year to December 2025. Unfortunately, that means its free cash flow fell significantly short of its reported profits. Indeed, in the last twelve months it reported free cash flow of €27m, which is significantly less than its profit of €80.5m. Notably, Theon International had negative free cash flow last year, so the €27m it produced this year was a welcome improvement. Notably, the company has issued new shares, thus diluting existing shareholders and reducing their share of future earnings.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

One essential aspect of assessing earnings quality is to look at how much a company is diluting shareholders. As it happens, Theon International issued 11% more new shares over the last year. Therefore, each share now receives a smaller portion of profit. To celebrate net income while ignoring dilution is like rejoicing because you have a single slice of a larger pizza, but ignoring the fact that the pizza is now cut into many more slices. Check out Theon International's historical EPS growth by clicking on this link.

How Is Dilution Impacting Theon International's Earnings Per Share (EPS)?

Theon International has improved its profit over the last three years, with an annualized gain of 168% in that time. But on the other hand, earnings per share actually fell by 23% per year. And in the last year the company managed to bump profit up by 19%. But in comparison, EPS only increased by 18% over the same period. So you can see that the dilution has had a bit of an impact on shareholders.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So it will certainly be a positive for shareholders if Theon International can grow EPS persistently. However, if its profit increases while its earnings per share stay flat (or even fall) then shareholders might not see much benefit. For the ordinary retail shareholder, EPS is a great measure to check your hypothetical "share" of the company's profit.

Our Take On Theon International's Profit Performance

As it turns out, Theon International couldn't match its profit with cashflow and its dilution means that earnings per share growth is lagging net income growth. Considering all this we'd argue Theon International's profits probably give an overly generous impression of its sustainable level of profitability. So while earnings quality is important, it's equally important to consider the risks facing Theon International at this point in time. You'd be interested to know, that we found 2 warning signs for Theon International and you'll want to know about them.

In this article we've looked at a number of factors that can impair the utility of profit numbers, and we've come away cautious. But there is always more to discover if you are capable of focussing your mind on minutiae. As finance nerds would already know, the accrual ratio from cashflow is a key measure for assessing how well a company's free cash flow (FCF) matches its profit. In plain english, this ratio subtracts FCF from net profit, and divides that number by the company's average operating assets over that period. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

That means a negative accrual ratio is a good thing, because it shows that the company is bringing in more free cash flow than its profit would suggest. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. To quote a 2014 paper by Lewellen and Resutek, "firms with higher accruals tend to be less profitable in the future".

data.title: If You Believe In Transformative AI Healthcare Then These Three Stocks Could Define Your Next Cycle
data.description:
Artificial intelligence in healthcare sits at the crossroads of rising inflation pressures, higher bond yields, and ongoing geopolitical tension, as investors search for themes tied to real-world problem solving and efficiency. AI tools that help doctors detect disease, personalise treatment, and reduce hospital bottlenecks speak directly to cost control and better patient outcomes, two priorities that tend to matter regardless of the growth or rate cycle. This article highlights three stocks from a Transformative AI Healthcare screener, providing a closer look at companies using data and algorithms to address diagnostics, treatment, and care delivery at scale.

Advertisement

GeneDx Holdings (WGS)

Overview: GeneDx Holdings is a genomics company that provides whole exome and whole genome sequencing for pediatric and rare diseases, using AI to analyze DNA and deliver clinical-grade test reports for rare disease, hereditary risk, and cancer. It also sells data and information services built on its large rare disease dataset.

Operations: GeneDx generates almost all of its US$427.5 million revenue in the United States, with US$423.1 million from its core Gene Dx segment and US$4.5 million from Fabric Genomics and legacy Sema4.

Market Cap: US$2.0b

GeneDx sits at the center of AI driven genomics in pediatrics, with whole exome and genome sequencing supported by a rare disease database and an AI platform that aims to make diagnosis faster and more precise. Analysts expect strong revenue and earnings growth. The stock currently trades well below some fair value estimates and price targets, which may appeal to investors who focus on mispriced growth. At the same time, the company is unprofitable, relies on external funding, and is seeing insider selling, so execution on reimbursement, volume growth, and cost discipline really matters. Recent deals, such as the Zevra NPC testing program and a new term loan, illustrate how GeneDx is trying to scale its model and strengthen its finances.

AI fueled genomics, a rare disease dataset and a stock trading below some fair value estimates can create an intriguing mismatch. See how the 3 key rewards and 1 important warning sign could reshape your view of the trade off hiding in GeneDx.

Pfizer (PFE)

Overview: Pfizer is a global pharmaceutical company that discovers, develops, manufactures, and sells prescription medicines and vaccines for conditions such as cardiovascular disease, migraine, cancer, rare diseases, infections, inflammation, and COVID 19, supported by partnerships with companies like Bristol Myers Squibb, Astellas, Merck KGaA, BioNTech, and others.

Operations: Pfizer generates the bulk of its US$62.2b revenue from its Global Biopharmaceuticals Business at US$61.2b, with smaller contributions from Pfizer Centreone at US$1.3b and Pfizer Ignite at US$41m, and earns US$37.1b in the United States versus US$25.5b across developed and emerging markets.

Market Cap: US$153.6b

Pfizer gives you exposure to artificial intelligence in healthcare through its work on biomolecular AI models, while still being anchored by a broad portfolio of medicines and vaccines in areas like oncology, rare disease, and metabolic health. The pipeline is busy, from obesity and GLP 1 programs and oncology data at ASCO 2026 to a Lyme disease vaccine candidate and priority reviews for cancer combinations. At the same time, the company is also dealing with earnings and revenue expected to decline over the next few years and questions around dividend coverage and debt. For investors weighing that mix of AI enabled R&D, late stage assets, pricing pressure, and patent risk, the full story goes well beyond headline P/E ratios and a high dividend yield.

Pfizer’s AI driven R&D and broad drug portfolio could be masking a very different risk reward profile than headline earnings suggest. Review the full 1 key reward and 4 important warning signs (3 are major!)

Insmed (INSM)

Overview: Insmed develops and commercializes therapies for serious and rare diseases, anchored by ARIKAYCE for difficult lung infections and a late stage pipeline that targets bronchiectasis, pulmonary hypertension, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and other hard to treat conditions across the United States, Europe, Japan, and additional regions.

Operations: Insmed generates US$606.4 million in revenue from developing and commercializing therapies for rare disease patients, with about US$453.0 million from the United States and US$153.5 million from international markets.

Market Cap: US$29.2b

Insmed sits at the intersection of AI driven protein engineering and late stage respiratory drugs. Brensocatib and ARIKAYCE label expansion are viewed as key drivers, alongside broader work in gene therapy and pulmonary hypertension. Analysts project revenue and earnings growth, yet the company is still loss making, carries a very high P/S multiple, and relies on external funding and ongoing share issuance, so execution on brensocatib launches, payer access, and Phase II and III readouts remains important. For investors who can tolerate clinical, regulatory, and dilution risk, the combination of rare disease focus, management continuity, and ambitious guidance on future respiratory revenues may make this a name to watch more closely as AI enabled drug development in complex lung conditions progresses.

Insmed’s AI driven respiratory push, rare disease focus, and high P/S valuation could be telling a very different story than the headlines suggest, and the analyst forecasts for Insmed may reveal the twist investors are missing
